I'm thinking in a painless way of doing this in both ends... what about creating `Entry` objects at socket patched mock runtime? 

Since `pook.Engine` is injected into the `MockEngine`, you can retrieve all the `pook` mocks from there via `engine.mocks`:
https://github.com/h2non/pook/blob/master/pook/engine.py#L39-L40
Then you can inspect the `pook.Mock` instance and compose the equivalent `Entry` objects before triggering any `mocket` layer.

The limitation I see with a hook approach is that `pook.Mock` can be defined in a lazy way since it provides a fluent API, therefore `pook` doesn't exactly knows when a `pook.Mock` is fully defined.

Do you think my proposal sounds viable on your end?

this is actually a Python language issue not specific to dpkt.

what you want is

   __hdr__ = (('a','B',0), )    # note the dangling comma

from http://docs.python.org/lib/typesseq.html :

"A single item tuple must have a trailing comma, such as (d,)."

using a list instead is fine too, and probably looks better anyway. :-)

There's definitely some weird stuff going on with `sep.Background`. It's not giving results I would expect for this image, and I do get a segfault for some parameter combinations.

I didn't notice you were using a multi-part index. For such a index, you can't feed data through a pipe. Minimap2 is designed this way to reduce peak memory. It is often impractical to hold either all target or all query sequences in memory. If you have enough memory, build a uni-part index by increasing `-I`.

Within a with statement data becomes the data of the child of the element, while `real_data` always is the data of the actual instance.
a simple example:
```python
doc = Document()
doc.append('This is the main document')
with doc.create(Section('One level)) as section:
    doc.append('This is the section')
    doc.real_data[0] # 'This is the main document'
    doc.data[0] # 'This is the section'
    doc[0] # 'This is the section'
    section[0] # 'This is the section'
```

I plan to rename the current matcher `body` to `raw_body` and add a new `body` matcher that'll inspect the *Content-Type* header and do the following:
* `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` -> `parse_qs`
* `application/json` -> `json.loads`
* `text/xml` and `xmlrpc` in *User-Agent* -> `xmlrpc_client.loads`
* otherwise, same as `raw_body`

This gives the flexibility to use the same cassette for various body types (compared to if you had `form_body`, `xmlrpc_body` and `json_body` matchers) and save the user some trouble wondering why sometimes it works and sometimes it doesn't due to Python ordering of dicts and sets, especially in Python 3.
On the downside, this might be too clever for its own good so maybe rename it `content_typed_body` or something like that and keep current `body` matcher unchanged.It can be considered a breaking change as much as a bug fix, it really depends on use cases.

Yeah, I'm going to have to agree with Kurt. Connection caching and indeed pooling of any limited resource is very, *very* dependent on the semantics of that resource. On the surface it seems like a commonly-necessitated feature: SQLAlchemy, requests, paramiko, SuPPort, and many others all use some form of pooling. But if one takes a real hard look at how libraries use these things, it varies widely. It was a challenge to find two libraries or applications where this would be a suitable drop-in strategy for both. Not that they don't exist, and not that this isn't a much more robust solution than ones I've used in the past, but I think it may exert too much influence on the architecture/strategy in an application's design.

And that brings me to the crux of the issue. `boltons` isn't a library of strategies. It's about more robust tactics and saving a few hastily hacked-together lines from causing bugs. It's nothing overarching like a concurrency or pooling strategy. With a name like `Pool`, there's not really a bright line on which concurrency strategies and protocols are supported. A generic pool is a cache, and the ones in cacheutils are about as complex as boltons was meant to get.

Just independently came to the same conclusion.

My Analysis:
1) The root cause of this problem is probably the fact that it is possible to change UNRAR_TOOL and not change other three dependent configurations. If NORMAL_CONFIG and ALT_CONFIG were, for example, namedtuples, that got assigned to global CURRENT_CONFIG this bug would be impossible.
2) Contributing cause is that changing UNRAR_TOOL from user-land is actually promoted as a normal way of interacting with the library. This is like doing surgery, and this bug just showed that UNRAR_TOOL was invisibly connected to the other config parts that start malfunctioning if we poke UNRAR_TOOL.

My contribution:
1) I sadly haven't got enough time to properly centralize all configuration, and it would be quite moronic of me to try to change the whole configuration system, after one poke at it lead to this bug. 
2) This is quite easy to fix, but it's only a stop-gap measure: remove UNRAR_TOOL from public API/documentation and add a function to correctly (I hope! I just print-debugged 3k lines of unknown code, so double-check me) modify it, like this:

```python
def set_unrar_path(new_unrar_path):
    """If new_unrar_path passes test - changes UNRAR_TOOL config"""
    global UNRAR_TOOL, OPEN_ARGS, EXTRACT_ARGS, TEST_ARGS

    OPEN_ARGS = ORIG_OPEN_ARGS
    EXTRACT_ARGS = ORIG_EXTRACT_ARGS
    TEST_ARGS = ORIG_TEST_ARGS

For any application that needs more GETCONFS, they'd need to enable a tor controlport filter profile for that application. That should then be a higher priority configuration file (lexically later file name) and overwrite the small config/names list with a longer one. Exotic combinations of random profiles may not be possible without profile modifications.

Profiles will always be per application. A one fits all may not be possible without sacrifice of security. Perhaps we could move away from per-application profiles and make that

- a) minimal, client only
- b) extended client only (not sure if there are any use cases yet)
- c) few add_onion related only
- d) more add_onion related commands
- e) even more relaxed
- f) everything

Or simpler, just two modes:

- a) minimal, client only
- b) more add_onion related commands

https://github.com/TheTorProject/ooni-probe/blob/feature/stream_bw/ooni/utils/tor.py

My use case requires that I can build multiple circuits that carry streams to the same destination, over different paths. I do this with the OnionRoutedTCPClientEndpoint, TorCircuitContextFactory, StreamAttacher, AttacherDispatcher classes. Changing path selection is as easy as subclassing StreamAttacher and implementing request_circuit_build. It's true that the examples provide a starting point, but I think that txtorcon should provide a API for specifying path selection at stream granularity. My code works - but I think it could probably be improved substantially - and it would be more useful to other developers if these features were available from txtorcon directly.

How this works:
In order to correctly map new streams to a specific circuit, the StreamAttacher must distinguish between streams, but as streams may have same destination, you need to use the source address and port to differentiate between streams. My OnionRoutedTCPEndpoint wraps the SOCKS5ClientFactory to learn the source ip, port before tor creates the stream. When the stream is created, the new stream event contains the stream_id and source ip, port that is used to match the stream id with the endpoint.  The OnionRoutedTCPClientEndpoint's TorCircuitContextFactory describes which StreamAttacher instance should be used.

The second feature is a way to easily plug in different StreamAttachers, simultaneously. This is done by a singleton proxy class AttacherDispatcher that proxies calls to attach_stream to a StreamAttacher instance. This is a workaround as txtorcon currently only supports a single stream attacher. This could be better engineered and implemented in txtorcon directly.

But the developers of wheel are already working on [this](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0513/). So stay tuned :)

A little background to this PR (it's a bit long, sorry). Based on (c)python 3 and psutil 5.4.0, I've conducted a little experiment. There are various ways of terminating a process in Python, and I wanted to see what can be observed by the parent afterwards. First, here is the script I used:

```py
import os
import psutil
import shlex
import signal
import subprocess
import sys
import time

def kill_with_os(proc):
    print('kill with os.kill(pid,SIGTERM)')
    os.kill(proc.pid, signal.SIGTERM)

def kill_with_subprocess(proc):
    print('kill child with subprocess.Popen.terminate()')
    proc.terminate()

def kill_with_psutil(proc):
    print('kill child with psutil.Process(pid).terminate()')
    psutil.Process(proc.pid).terminate()

def do_not_kill(proc):
    pass

def run_and_kill_child(killfn, procarg):
    print('run child with %s' % procarg)
    with subprocess.Popen([sys.executable, __file__, procarg]) as proc:
        time.sleep(1)
        killfn(proc)
        proc.wait()
    print('child terminated with %s' % proc.returncode)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print('parent process started')
        run_and_kill_child(kill_with_os, 'os')
        run_and_kill_child(kill_with_subprocess, 'subprocess')
        run_and_kill_child(kill_with_psutil, 'psutil')
        run_and_kill_child(do_not_kill, 'return')
    else:
        print('child process started with %s' % sys.argv[1])
        while sys.argv[1] != 'return':
            pass
```

So, it uses `kill` from the `os` module, `terminate` from `subprocess`, and `terminate` from `psutil`. The next listing is the output I got on macOS 10.12 (and I think I would get the same on Linux, too):

```
$ python3 killtest.py 
parent process started
run child with os
child process started with os
kill with os.kill(pid,SIGTERM)
child terminated with -15
run child with subprocess
child process started with subprocess
kill child with subprocess.Popen.terminate()
child terminated with -15
run child with psutil
child process started with psutil
kill child with psutil.Process(pid).terminate()
child terminated with -15
run child with return
child process started with return
child terminated with 0
```

And this is what I got on Windows 10:

```
>py -3 killtest.py
parent process started
run child with os
child process started with os
kill with os.kill(pid,SIGTERM)
child terminated with 15
run child with subprocess
child process started with subprocess
kill child with subprocess.Popen.terminate()
child terminated with 1
run child with psutil
child process started with psutil
kill child with psutil.Process(pid).terminate()
child terminated with 0
run child with return
child process started with return
child terminated with 0
```

It can be seen that on a Posix platform, `terminate` calls are in sync with `kill(,SIGTERM)`, i.e., exit code of the process is always `-SIGTERM`, and most importantly non-0. However, on Windows, the observable exit code after psutils's `terminate` differs both from `kill(,SIGTERM)` and from subprocess's `terminate`, and quite confusingly, yields 0, which normally signals the end of successful process execution.

Note: of course, it cannot be expected on Windows to get `-SIGTERM` as exit code, the platform is simply not capable of a negative exit code. But then, `+SIGTERM` seems to be an acceptable alternative.

Note 2: the approach of the standard `subprocess` module - i.e., it terminates the process with exit code 1 - is a bit questionable to me, but at least it does not yield 0.

About @desbma proposal:

It might seem a good idea at first glance but there are two main problems:

- using `partition` will only split the string at the first occurrence of the given argument (the process name). So the above example will be:

> /Applications/**Google Chrome**.app/Contents/MacOS/Google Chrome

Imo, that would be good as well.

- process name as sentinel: it becomes a problem when the it appears more than once in the full cmdline. For instance:

> **glances** /bin/python3.4 /home/alessio/venvs/py34-glances/bin/glances
> /home/alessio/venvs/py34-**glances** /bin/python3.4 /home/alessio/venvs/py34-glances/bin/glances

The correct cmdline:
> /home/alessio/venvs/py34-glances/bin/python3.4 /home/alessio/venvs/py34-glances/bin/glances

---
Agree with @nicolargo, I don't think that psutil would consider the truncated name a bug.

For the record: the current highlighting implementation is not based on the process name, so the psutil bug may not be related.

Good remarks, @ultrabug. 

### Icon fonts

I've added a hint to _look into icon fonts_ in the docstring, and I don't think we should put more details, at least not in there. Ideally it could be a wiki page with the full tutorial, and telling from experience, it's going to be neither small nor easy to write :smiley:.

To clarify this particular change, previously the `blocks` and `charging_character` variables had _hardcoded_ values `"_▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█"` and `"⚡"` respectively. I've only made them configurable, preserving the those hardcoded values as defaults. 

Now if people want, they can for example set `blocks` variable to be `"LMH"`, and `L` letter would be shown when the battery level is low, `M` when it's medium and `H` when it's high. 

If a person happens to use an icon font (let's take FontAwesome as an example), they will have to check the unicode codes of icons in that particular font and compose the `blocks` and `charging_character` variables out of them. 
> Since we took FontAwesome as an example, [the icon representing half of the battery ~> "" <~](https://fortawesome.github.io/Font-Awesome/icon/battery-half/) has a unicode code of `f242` - you would see it here if you have the font installed.

In my particular case, I have created a font on my own, thus I chose which icons correspond to which unicode codes, and based on that I configured my `blocks` and `charging_character` variables. Therefore this part of my config is useless to anyone else, unless they also install my font.

------

As you can see, this very quickly becomes complicated to explain and to provide a single set of steps, that would suit everyone. That's why I want to keep it simple, as it is today.

Please have a look, do you agree that this is sufficient?

### Implement the proper format string

I can certainly help refactoring the `battery_level` module, but I would like to make it in a separate pull request. I anticipate that to be a bigger change, with more questions to be resolved -- while this pull request on contrast is a small improvement, that doesn't affect current users of the module.

The primary question that I have is about backward compatibility, what is your strategy in this project? 

> 1. I think the whole concept of `modes` in this plugin becomes obsolete, if there is a proper support for `format` parameter.
> 2. The parameter `show_percent_with_blocks` will also be useless.
> 3. I would give the `percentage` a more descriptive format than the current `{}`.

The question is, do we want to keep backward compatibility, or rather get rid of everything obsolete completely during the refactoring?

> I thought that a Stream object would implicitly yield the control flow to the the event loop after each iteration (as is the case in workaround 2), but that seems to not always be the case. Is this by design or a perhaps a bug?

Not yielding after each iteration is by design, but the issue you describe is definitely a bug. I guess we could give the control back to the event loop right before yielding the value for all the stream operators (in `Streamer.__anext__` for instance), but I'm afraid it would affect the performance too much. Instead, I'd rather fix `from_iterable` (see commit 514759e).

The generator already finishes up since `aclose` actually throws a `GeneratorExit` into the generator, meaning the `finally` statements will be executed as expected.

Sure, if you think it's worth it.

Thank you for taking the time to write such a detailed response. :)

### My use case

I'm working on a password generator similar to [supergenpass](https://github.com/chriszarate/supergenpass). I need an algorithm that generates unique, strong passwords based on a strong master key.

The challenge is that pseudorandomly generated data short enough to be a password (e.g. taking the first 10 characters of base64'd data) is often not "random" enough. For example, you might get something like `BBaaaaaa` or `123woman`.

To combat this, instead of just generating 1 password I'm generating hundreds of candidate passwords until one matching all security criteria is found. Because this is basically a form of keystretching HKDF seems to be the perfect choice. HKDF also has the advantage of being simple to implement in other languages which is useful for porting.

I've also considered PBKDF2 with a count of 1, but that violates the minimum iteration count of 1000 and basically makes PBKDF2 a less secure version of HKDF (such as described in its own comparison with other algorithms).

### About security and compatibility

> 2- Security: I'm not sure if the security proofs (http://eprint.iacr.org/2010/264.pdf) depend on the fixed length of the counter.

[The paper](https://eprint.iacr.org/2010/264.pdf) referenced in the RFC states (page 10):

> the counter i is non-wrapping and of a given fixed size, e.g., a single byte

So according to the author any size counter is fine, as long as it is fixed size. 

> 1- Compatibility: other implementations of HKDF will not support counter values > 255, e.g. https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6234#section-8.4

You are correct, I haven't found any other implementations with bigger counters. However, the 8bit variant still has the same output, and for the other bitcounts we're essentially changing nothing except for the size of the counter. So any other implementations with the same counter size should produce the same result, as long as they use the same endianness. For this, network order (big endian) seems a good choice but we could make it configurable (by adding more COUNTER variants) if there is ever demand for that.

The `namedtuple` is the one we use now, for example:

```
class Block(namedtuple("Block", "header transactions")):
    pass
```

The `namedtuple and slots` is the same as a `namedtuple` with the addition of `__slots__ = ()` to prevent the dynamic additions of new attibutes, for example:

```
class Block(namedtuple("Block", "header transactions")):
    __slots__ = ()
```

The `slots` is the standard definition of `__slots__`, for example:

```
class Block(object):
    __slots__ = ("header",  "transactions")

    def __init__(self, header,  transactions):
        self.header = header
        self.transactions = transactions
```

For the object sizes I used `pympler.asizeof`.

>>> name.title=""

@maxcountryman I think this is a similar problem to the one I addressed back in 0.2.10.

The way you have the strong session protected implemented causes Flask-Login to declare the session invalid everytime a client that is not logged in sends a request. This is because the session has no `_id`, so the conditional [here](https://github.com/maxcountryman/flask-login/blob/51defb5c3ecf498ec5040c80976f32e782cfc343/flask_login.py#L366) is true.

As a result of declaring the session invalid, Flask-Login clears it, but not only it clears its own data from the session, it wipes it out completely. This creates a conflict with Flask-WTF, which stores the form's CSRF token in the session. So essentially with the current version it is impossible to submit a Flask-WTF form if the user is not logged in.

Maybe Flask-Login should only clear its own variables from the session and leave the remaining data alone?

There's an op in NDArray which can benefit from fill https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/versions/master/api/python/ndarray.html?highlight=full#mxnet.ndarray.full. Currently it is done in the frontend through `empty` and in-place assignment. And this op is not included in symbol (though it's doable through `ones` and multiply). It's worth considering the `full` use case in this PR since doing it with fill will be faster, though it wouldn't be compatible with the current template implementation of `set_to`.

All in all, I really like the functionality but please make it DagRun based.

I think you're bumping up against a non-goal for Bleach. Bleach is intended for cleaning fragments rather than entire HTML documents. Bleach uses HTMLParser's `parseFragment` (https://html5lib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/html5lib.html#html5lib.html5parser.HTMLParser.parseFragment) which won't try to add all the missing tags to make a well-formed document rather than `parse` (https://html5lib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/html5lib.html#html5lib.html5parser.HTMLParser.parse) which does.

If you need to clean entire documents, you could probably write your own `Cleaner` class that uses `parse` rather than `parseFragment`. That happens on this line:

https://github.com/mozilla/bleach/blob/master/bleach/sanitizer.py#L133

I think this is right. The name attribute of a trait is used as "what is this trait called as an attribute of the HasTraits instance it belongs to". The trait instances for typechecking the elements in a container are not an attribute on a HasTraits instance, so it doesn't make sense for `name` to be set on them.

If I was redesigning traitlets from scratch, I would make a clearer separation between the type checking parts and handling trait values (i.e. defaults, change notifications). Using traits for the elements in a list only involves the former part.
Use `TraitType(read_only=True)`.

Btw, I have pushed a relevant commit about `utils.sentinel.Sentinel` ignoring copy/deepcopy.
Sentinels must not change, or cloned traits fail defaults 
(a a really nasty bug discovered a year ago, please don't ask me more).

This has broken widgets because it calls notify_change with events that are not change events. We either need to ensure that these events are supersets of change events, or trigger a different call that `notify_change`

I started to consider this as the way to approach notifier types in https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/pull/111 where trait types would be responsible for generating subchanges iff there was a `change` (i.e. `old != new`).

The intended behaviour is as follows:

1.  The `region_constraint` is an optional parameter, applicable only to risk calculations, which defines the polygon that will be used for filtering the assets from the exposure model.

    The vertices of the polygon are listed in the Well-known text (WKT) format:
 `region_constraint = lon_1 lat_1, lon_2 lat_2, ..., lon_n lat_n`

    If the specified polygon is not already topologically closed (i.e., the last point is not the same as the first point), it should be closed during validation.

2. Asset filtering based on the `region_constraint`:

  * If the `region_constraint` **is not provided**, all assets in the exposure model are considered for the risk calculation.

  * If the `region_constraint` **is provided**, all assets in the exposure model located outside the polygon defined by the `region_constraint` are discarded; only those *assets within the polygon* are considered for the risk calculation.

3. The `asset_hazard_distance` is an optional parameter, applicable only to risk calculations, which defines a search radius (in km) that will be used for associating a hazard input site to each asset.

    If this parameter **is not provided**, a default value of *5 km* is used as the `asset_hazard_distance`.

    For each asset considered for the risk calculation (obtained after the filtering in Step 2):

    - If no hazard input site is found within the radius defined by the `asset_hazard_distance`, the asset is skipped and a message is provided mentioning the id of the asset that is affected by this issue. See note in the following comment regarding the handling of this case in an elegant manner.

    - If multiple hazard input sites are found within the radius defined by the `asset_hazard_distance`, the hazard input site with the shortest distance from the asset location is associated with the asset. It is acceptable for the associated hazard input site to be located outside the polygon defined by the `region_constraint`.

For boto3, we avoid customizations that would better be implemented service side. For now, you can use the same behavior by adding a step that looks something like this:


```python
region = 'us-west-2'
jar = 's3://{region_name}.elasticmapreduce/libs/script-runner/script-runner.jar'
args = 's3://{region_name}.elasticmapreduce/libs/state-pusher/0.1/fetch'
debugging_step = {
    'Name': 'Setup Hadoop Debugging',
    'ActionOnFailure': 'TERMINATE_JOB_FLOW',
    'HadoopJarStep': {
        'Jar': jar.format(region_name=region),
        'Args': [args.format(region_name=region)]
    }
}
```

Hello,

Great tool, first time reporter here and long time heavy user of many other package managers (npm/yarn, nuget/paket, bower, conan, gem, old-style-pip, cargo and probably a few others, but more casually).

As I understand properly, the virtualenv creation (something like : `python -m virtualenv . --python=3.6`) installs some packages by default (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`) and we don't seem to be able to override the versions of these packages directly from that command (and the `python -m virtualenv .` command usually always pulls the three dependencies close to latest). That is limitation we must play along with, since this is upstream-behavior.

Those three packages (an maybe others) are exceptions and we need to handle them carefully.

The behavior I would expect from `Pipenv` if I was to specify something like "`setuptools=XYZ`" in the Pipfile would be one of the following : 
1. Fail, and fail hard with an error message stating you can't specify a pinned dependency on `setuptools`. (Or at least, warn very loudly). This would avoid the case of trying to pin one of those exception-packages, expecting it pinned and debugging for a few hours before finally finding out `virtualenv` via `pipenv` silently installed a different version than the one you specified. It removes the illusion of control over these packages. These packages should not be pinned anyways, so it enforces the users to have clean Pipfiles
2. Override the `setuptools` to the version specified in the `Pipfile`. e.g. with something like `pipenv run install setuptools=XYZ`. This way, the users of `Pipenv` have a standard/coherent interface across the normal-packages AND the exception-packages. They don't need to know what is happening under the hood to get their packages to the version they want.
3. Other suggestions?

Thanks again for creating and maintaining this tool!

- Frank

For workers, we don't open up ssh tunnels/use the context manager and just use fabric's SSH facilities directly https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/master/streamparse/ext/fabric.py#L80-L100. In order to properly support users, think we should do the following:

1. Modify your config param to be `ssh_tunnel_nimbus: true|false` which makes it explicit that this only controls whether we try to SSH tunnel to Nimbus or connect directly.
2. Modify our use of the `user` param from config.json which uses the same user for both tunneling to Nimbus and sshing to workers (an assumption that doesn't hold up).  We could add `user_nimbus` and `user_worker` but we'd be duplicating info that should already be in a user's ssh config.  Allow user's to drop this setting which will instead enable fabric's `env.use_ssh_config = True` in here https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/master/streamparse/ext/fabric.py.

This allows us to properly support a more complex SSH config setup while hopefully not completely breaking streamparse for current users.

The one thing that worries me with this approach is that the use of the local SSH config is not immediately transparent to users but an explicit `use_ssh_config` param in config.json may be overkill.

Thoughts?

For those familiar with the `rank.core_cy._core` algorithm, here is mine:

*Note*: `img` should be padded beforehand `p = selem_size//2; np.pad(img, ((p+1,p),(p,p)))`
```
@jit(nogil=True, nopython=True)
def _pop_bilateral(img, hist, selem_size, out, s0=10, s1=10):
    "Sliding window histogram algo"
    iy, ix = img.shape
    pad = selem_size//2
    # initialize histogram
    for ii in range(selem_size):
        for jj in range(selem_size):
            val = img[ii, jj]
            hist[val] += 1
    
    # every pixel in zick zack
    for i in range(1, iy-2*pad): # rows
        if i%2 == 1: # zick
            r = range(ix-2*pad)
        else: # zack
            r = range(ix-2*pad-1, -1, -1)
        
        for j in r: # cols
            # update hist
            if ((j == 0 and i%2 == 1) or
                (j == ix-2*pad-1 and i%2 == 0)): # row step
                for jj in range(selem_size):
                    v1 = img[i-1, j+jj]
                    v2 = img[i+selem_size-1, j+jj]
                    hist[v1] -= 1
                    hist[v2] += 1
            elif i%2 == 1: # column step forward
                for ii in range(selem_size):
                    v1 = img[i+ii, j-1]
                    v2 = img[i+ii, j+selem_size-1]
                    hist[v1] -= 1
                    hist[v2] += 1
            else: # column step backward
                for ii in range(selem_size):
                    v1 = img[i+ii, j+selem_size]
                    v2 = img[i+ii, j]
                    hist[v1] -= 1
                    hist[v2] += 1
                
            # get out value
            val = img[i+pad, j+pad]
            o = 0
            for h in range(val-s0, val+s1+1):
                if h < 0 or h > 255:
                    continue
                o += hist[h]
            out[i-1, j] = o
```

Thank you all to for the review, with a special mention to @jni for detecting a silly bug that I had introduced. There is now a test to check that the output of ``_blob_overlap`` against the analytical formula.

However, when looking for a good test for this issue, I stumbled across (what I consider to be) a bug, which I didn't introduce. Indeed, if you have two overlapping circles, it's almost impossible to have the algorithm returning the two blobs, even with a large value of overlap. Example:
```
>>> img = np.zeros((256, 256))
>>> xs, ys = circle(120, 120, 20)
>>> img[xs, ys] = 1
>>> xs, ys = circle(100, 120, 20)
>>> img[xs, ys] = 1
>>> feature.blob_log(img,  min_sigma=10, max_sigma=30, overlap=0.6)
array([[ 110.        ,  120.        ,   18.88888889]])
```
The actual overlap between the two disks is 0.39, but since the algorithm detects a larger blob centered between the two circles, it keeps only this one (since the overlap between this fake blob and the real circles is much larger than the overlap between the two circles).

> Reading from on-disk data stores: Memory is something of a problem for me. a typical model that I run might eat up 10+ GB of RAM

Are you aware that patsy has full support for building design matrices in bounded memory, by doing multiple passes over the data? You call [`incr_dbuilder`](https://patsy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.incr_dbuilder) or similar to scan the data to figure out which columns are numerical vs. categorical, the levels of categoricals, the mean and stddev if you're using `standardize`, etc. -- basically everything that requires looking at the whole data set -- and it gives you back a `DesignInfo`, and then you can pass that `DesignInfo` to `dmatrix` along with a horizontal slice of your data, and it gives you back a horizontal slice of the design matrix. It's maybe not the most convenient thing to use, but AFAIK it's everything you'd need to e.g. write `dask.patsy`.

> Partial predictions

Does [`DesignInfo.subset`](https://patsy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.DesignInfo.subset) do what you want? (You still have to pick what values you want to predict at etc., I'm not sure how much patsy can really help there.)

> Categorical grouping

I'm not sure what this means. Is this a standard technique? Any references to what the resulting design matrices would look like?

> Weights: For methods like standardize, it may make sense to weight the observations

True! You can always define your own [stateful transform](https://patsy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/stateful-transforms.html#stateful-transforms) but adding features like this to `standardize` seems handy.

Hi Arun,

This is part of the new API for OpticalPSF, which has stricter requirements for how to set the scale (in meters) for an image of the pupil plane.  Sorry that I didn't think about how this might affect the wfirst module.  From the `galsim.Aperture` documentation:

```
The pixel scale of the pupil plane can be specified in one of three ways.  In descending order 
of priority, these are:
      1.  The `pupil_plane_scale` keyword argument (units are meters).
      2.  The `pupil_plane_im.scale` attribute (units are meters).
      3.  If (1) and (2) are both None, then the scale will be inferred by assuming that the
          illuminated pixel farthest from the image center is at a physical distance of self.diam/2.
```

In this case, the image file of the WFIRST focal plane has a scale of 1.0 (I'm not sure if this is from the WCS or if it's a default), so the 4096x4096 image is interpreted as having physical extent of ~4km x ~4km.  While it would certainly be cool to have a ~km aperture optical/infrared telescope in space, I don't think this is in the plans for WFIRST!

Of the three ways listed above for setting the correct scale, I think in this case setting it as an image attribute (via the image file header WCS) makes the most sense.  (Anyone know if it's possible to indicate in the FITS header that the WCS units are in meters?)

That said, it would probably be a good idea to check in the code if the inferred diameter coming from the pupil image + scale at least roughly matches the diameter specified as an argument, and report a helpful warning if they don't.

Let me know if you want to tackle this one, or if you'd like me to.  If you want me to though, I'll need to know the precise pixel scale for the WFIRST pupil plane image.

Before tackling specific improvements, the best bet would be to develop a self-contained example code that you want to optimize.  Then we can actually profile it and see what the tall poles are for optimization.  I'm always happy to add efficiency improvements, but without a target use case to profile, my experience is that one tends to focus on things that aren't actually helpful.

Aside: for very fast model fitting using GalSim, my guess is that you will be better off doing the modeling in k-space rather than real space.  Then you don't need any FFTs.  For simple models, GalSim draws the k-space image in <~ 1ms typically.  cf. https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/issues/873#issuecomment-287159168.  I.e. FFT your data once and generate models in k-space, rather than repeatedly FFT the models to compare to the data.

Furthermore, it doesn't returns data in rows, making features such as pagination very difficult to implement.

Re models: if you don't specify a model_class in Database.select(), an ad-hoc model will be created according to the column definitions returned by the query. This also means that the values will be converted to their Pythonic types.

If you don't even need this, you can use Database.raw() to get the query result as text. Then you could iterate over the lines in the text and call utils.parse_tsv() to convert each line into a list of string values.

I want to share my workaround for the issue:

```python
class RawOperator(SimpleOperator):
    """
    Workaround for raw sql in conditions
    """

    def to_sql(self, model_cls, field_name, value):
        if field_name == 'raw':
            return '(%s)' % value
        field = getattr(model_cls, field_name)
        value = field.to_db_string(field.to_python(value, pytz.utc))
        return ' '.join([field_name, self._sql_operator, value])


register_operator('eq', RawOperator('='))

# How to use it
raw_condition = 'pointInPolygon((latitude, longitude), %s)' % coords
SomeModel.objects_in(db).filter(raw=raw_condition)
```

Here, you have two separate sets of parameters that you'd like to update at different times. By default DyNet updates all of the parameters in the ParameterCollection every time you call trainer (except for LookupParameters, which are updated sparsely). In order to change this behavior, you can use the `add_subcollection()` function of ParameterCollection (http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python_ref.html#parametercollection-and-parameters) to create two parameter subcollections, then define two different trainers, one for each subcollection. When you want to update the parameters of each subcollection, you can do so by calling the update function of the particular trainer.

@kirknorth A lot has changed in Py-ART since this was submitted which makes merging this difficult.  Sorry I did not get to this earlier.

Looking at the code, I'm seeing three major changes that this PR introduces.

1. `grid_from_radars` can produce non-uniform grids.
2. Trapp and Doswell (2000) weighting.
3. Use pyproj to determine a more accurate offset between radars.

I think, 1 needs to wait until the **Grid** object is refactored to support both uniform and inon-uniform grids.  Being able to generate non-uniform grids would be great but if you cannot do anything with them... then not so great.

Number 2 should get included in Py-ART in both the `map_to_grid` and `map_gates_to_grid` function.  Perhaps having three build in weighting functions (Cressman, "Traditional" Barnes, and "Trapp and Doswell" Barnes) would be reasonable.

Using pyproj for better mapping between lat/lon and Cartesian space is an big bug in Py-ART and should be fixed, but I think the issues needs to be address in a more comprehensive manner than is done here.  This is especially true if we wish to keep basemap and pyproj as optional dependencies.  Issue #294 for more discussion on this topic.  

My recommendation would be that two issues get created which refer to this PR; one to add Trapp and Doswell type weighting to the gridding methods.  The second requesting support the creation and use of non-uniform grid.  Issue #294 already covers the projection issues.  With these two issues created I think this PR can be closed.  Does that sound reasonable?

Obviously, longterm: you don't want to kick off one pipeline per namespace permanently, you lose the major benefits of pipeline fan-out abortion and success if you aren't yielding child pipelines.

You are also correct about the default namespace - it would be the namespace of the process that created the task, for any of the pipeline tasks `complete` `fanout` `abort` etc.

-

A much more comfortable solution, now that I'm seeing a larger scope here, would involve keeping ALL pipeline entities in the namespace of the root pipeline. Then, if you need namespace switching, you would explicitly achieve this per pipeline.

This also simplifies the answer to "How do I find a pipeline with a given `pipeline_id`?".

One paradigm I use a lot is class inheritance for pipelines with a common setup function, to prepare for any common variables. This is good for your larger pipeline chains, like I believe you may have.

```python
class MyRootPipeline(Pipeline):
    def setup(self, **kwargs):
        """Perform setup for MyRootPipeline and all derivative pipes."""
        # Get pipeline information, and do setup.
        self.namespace = kwargs.get('namespace', None)
        self.kwargs = kwargs.copy()  # changes to kwargs shouldn't affect local copy

        if self.namespace:
            # Sets the namespace for the current HTTP request.
            namespace_manager.set_namespace(self.namespace)

    def run(self, **kwargs):
        # Do your setup
        self.setup(**kwargs)

        # Do stuff
        
        # Yield child with same kwargs, and any additional args.
        kwargs['namespace'] = "other_namespace"
        yield ChildPipeline(child_wants_candy=True, **kwargs)

class ChildPipeline(MyRootPipeline):
    """Subclassed from MyRootPipeline for common setup procedure."""

    def run(self, child_wants_candy, **kwargs):
        # Performs setup, switches namespace, ...
        self.setup(**kwargs)

        # Stuff and things in the new namespace.
```

This won't work now, until the pipeline knows to explicitly use the namespace for the yielded child pipeline and callback tasks, etc. - but that's the support I'd consider targeting for this issue.

The issue I'm seeing in master is that a blob of stdout data without a header is being placed on the socket:
```
 6.1s recv(<pants.java.nailgun_client.NoisyClientSock object>, 5)
 6.6s -> 'Welco'
 6.6s recv(<pants.java.nailgun_client.NoisyClientSock object>, 1466264675)
 6.8s -> 'me to Scala 2.11.11 (Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Ser...xpressions for evaluation. Or try :help.\n\nscala> ' (file:///var/folders/tc/kq93y4pd5jn6gy3pfzd2zd5h0000gn/T/q31418051.txt)
```
... I can't locate the writer of the value on the server, even tracing aggressively using `q`, and proxy-instrumenting the socket instance to ensure that only instrumented methods are called.

----

At this point, I can only assume that this is a concurrency issue due to the use of forked processes writing to those file-like objects in a thread-unsafe way. There are two things that don't quite align with this assumption though:
1. It's extremely consistent... it doesn't "look" like a race.
2. Even if it were a race, I would expect to be able to locate the code accessing the socket due to the `q` logging.

I believe the root cause occurs at [line 199 in multithread.py](https://github.com/Azure/azure-data-lake-store-python/blob/82f319fb045286f6cf953ce7510568574af1666a/azure/datalake/store/multithread.py#L199):
`file_pairs = [(os.path.join(self.lpath, os.path.relpath(f['name'] +'.inprogress', local_rel_rpath)), f)`

Specifically, `os.path.join()` shouldn't contain latter arguments that start with a slash (i.e., local_rel_rpath).  If this occurs, the arg is interpreted as an absolute path and overwrites the preceding arguments.
[https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1945920/why-doesnt-os-path-join-work-in-this-case](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1945920/why-doesnt-os-path-join-work-in-this-case)

`Connector.close()` should be converted to coroutine, we'll do it in upcoming releases.
Just after `transport.close()` call a paired protocol is not in finished state (`connection_lost()` is no called).
For plain TCP transport it will happen on next event loop iteration, with SSL transport the situation is much worse: TLS shutdown requires passing data over TLS socket.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

I have written this up as a blog post as well: http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html

I had the application deployed to a folder, and had set up a virtual directory against the parent - path-wise it was a child folder also. Leaving it as a virtual directory, or letting IIS resolve the path resulted in the 502.5. 

So I converted it to an application. And it works...

It is worth pointing out though, that the application is still using the parent application pool - it does not have its own.

Having said that, the root/parent is a blank site - there is no web.config at that level. One small issue I see is it is assuming the client will never have to complete a DVSNI challenge for renewal. This is probably a pretty good assumption right now, but there is nothing in the ACME spec preventing this from occurring. If the client did have to complete a DVSNI challenge, it would be unable to do so as the web server will be bound to port 443.

To get around this, we could have the user also register a command to stop their web server. To complete a DVSNI challenge, the client would stop the web server, perform the DVSNI challenge, and then restart the web server (possibly using a third registered command?). This is much less elegant and may not be necessary, but this approach is relying on always being able to use SimpleHTTP challenge to satisfy DV.

>The client is using background threads and they will not be available in a forked child process, so you will need to instantiate each client in the fork where it will run. ' mean . 

Each time a client is instantiated the following threads are created: 
  - rdkafka_main 
  - broker_thread (per broker)

Each broker thread has access to a pointer for the application you originally instantiated. When you call fork however you create a new process which has a new address and thus none of the brokers have are aware of the new client. I'll look into it more closely, but I'm strongly inclined _not_ to do this. `pytz` is a separate project, and there's no reason for us to program against their API. If you want to use `pytz` time zones, you should probably just localize them yourself after they've been parsed.

I think I'd be much more amenable to a solution taking advantage of the fact that `tzinfos` can be callable, perhaps one where tzinfos first tries calling with 3 arguments (tzname, tzoffset, parsed_date), and if that fails, tries calling it with 2 arguments (tzname, tzoffset). That way, you are free to incorporate `localize` into a `tzinfos` function.

That said, I'm hoping the improvements I suggested in #125 would alleviate this at least to some degree, since the end user would have access to the timezone object and all the date components before it returns a date, and they would be free to inject timezone localization as they see fit.

You raise fair points @davidism.

The rationale for the current API is as follows:

- The Schema decorators--`preprocessor` and `data_handler`--allow multiple Schemas to use the same pre- and post-processing routines without having to inherit from base classes or mixins. They also hide the fact that multiple functions can be stored within a list.
- `make_object` is a method because is is meant to be specific to a Schema. Whereas pre- and post-processors are written as generic functions, `make_object` provides a 1-to-1 mapping between a schema and an "app-level" object.
- There is no preprocessing hook for dumping/serialization because marshmallow is not meant to make updates to app-level objects. All processing happens in your application's business logic *before* serialization.

Here is the intended flow:

```
[client input] -> preprocess/validate -> make object -> [app-level object] -> business logic* -> [app-level-object] -> serialize -> modify serialized data
```

That said, I am not entirely opposed to changing the API if it is unclear. Perhaps you could describe your use case in more detail and we can discuss possible solutions.

So far I have two use cases for list_ports:
* get a list of port and human readable descriptions, used e.g. in a GUI drop-down box for the user to select a serial port
* use hwgrep://NAME to match one or two specific USB-serial device types

I don't think i've ever used the resulting list of tuples in an application for something else. The first case is solved by using the strings as-is, and the second by using list_ports.grep() or by use of the hwgrep:// URL form and serial_for_url.

Therefore I do not yet see the advantage of having a dictionary as result.

you've added a third one
* firmware update on (USB) devices where the device alters its ID during the upgrade, identify the device in both modes

Though in my experience that one is not such a big problem. As usually only one device is updated at a time, there is only one with a different identification, specific to the updater. So the update tool can easily identify the devices in both modes. And other operating systems may not provide such a location so relying on it is not possible when writing cross platform applications.


* One improvement could be to add the location to the 3rd string in the tuple. Add as many useful identification properties as there are available...
* Yes, a named tuple is probably a good idea (now that we only need to support Python versions that have it)
* A middle ground could also be to use a dedicated result class that acts like a tuple of 3 items when accessing via index but has access to single fields with getattr. e.g. `port, desc, hwid = result` and `result.SNR`, `result.VID` etc. (The stanard libs' `urlsplit` has something similar as result)  (+1)

I'm actually mistaken -- most of the logic should be in `Layer.add_param`, so we could modify that function to store the initializers somewhere. So it is a more local change than I thought at first.

However, Lasagne allows different ways to specify initializers, and we would need to clearly define what it means to "reset" all of them:
* initialization function: this one is easy, just call the function again to generate a new set of values
* explicit initial value (numpy array): just reuse the initial value
* Theano shared variable or expression: ???

I don't really know what the correct way is to handle that last one. I think there is basically no clean solution for that. We can't really change the contents of the shared variables as they may also be used elsewhere that we don't know of.

So that means `reset_params()` would not work for any nets with parameter sharing, for example... which is a bit icky. So I think I'm still not in favour of adding it. Wrapping the net in a class or function is probably the best solution.

That's an interesting one. `%load` and similar commands like `%recall` break the usual execution model by modifying the input. I guess they might also not do the expected thing if you 'run all' in the notebook.

Perhaps a better way to do it would be to check the [execute_reply payloads](https://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/messaging.html#payloads-deprecated) for a `set_next_input` payload, and if one is found, run the code from that without checking its output (since there's presumably no equivalent cell in the reference notebook).

It probably will be one of the last. The reason this parameter was a problem is that it was only being run on import time. As I've been arguing for years now, we should not be changing the state of the program at import if we can possible avoid it.

This isn't "the plugin fixing the tests", this is "the plugin was doing something slightly silly and is now doing it in a more sensible way". We're not changing how the config is "set up", we're just changing at which point a global config value is set.

Your idea about tearing down the config at shutdown would not help this bug as the code which sets the value would still only be run once per interpreter session.

In the non-MPI version of PPO data is aggregated across workers, just like you are describing. Specifically, the batch size is set to N * T in the current implementation, here: https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/bdd4d385a654f65f9cc5b7c43eba3d5f27574a8b/baselines/ppo2/ppo2.py#L224
(nenv is number of environments = number of parallel workers that interact with environment)

In the MPI version, same thing happens, except gradients are also aggregated across the MPI workers (by using MpiAdamOptimizer, herehttps://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/bdd4d385a654f65f9cc5b7c43eba3d5f27574a8b/baselines/ppo2/ppo2.py#L52), so the effective batch size is Nmpi * N * T. 
In either case, the batch size is not limited by T. 

Hope this helps!

@ohcibi
> could you elaborate your response a bit? As I said that 'application creation' is one of the things that causes the most confusion here 8-).

He means: 
  * when you create a `oauth2_provider.models.Application` instance (which will save a Django model in your application's database)
  * you set `my_application.authorization_grant_type = Application.GRANT_PASSWORD` (see [relevant](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/b4378b3d3197c4fb7b13b0f7ba624616f4241f28/oauth2_provider/models.py#L47) [source code](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/b4378b3d3197c4fb7b13b0f7ba624616f4241f28/oauth2_provider/models.py#L47))

which marks this `Application` instance as using the Resource Owner Password Credentials flow. It's even easier when you use Django's Admin interface to create the Application. Just choose "Resource owner password-based" from the dropdown for the "Authorization grant type" field. Basically you run something like this:

```python
>>> from oauth2_provider.models import Application
>>> Application.objects.create(authorization_grant_type=Application.GRANT_PASSWORD, client_type=Application.CLIENT_PUBLIC)
```

**Notes for your implementation:**

With a single-page application you probably also want to set `my_application.client_type = Application.CLIENT_PUBLIC`, because the `client_secret` would be easily stolen. Regardless, you'll have to send at least the `client_id` of your `Application` instance in the `Authorization` HTTP header (or inside the POST body) as described in [OAuth2 Section 2.3 (link to spec)](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-2.3).

You then hook up the `TokenView` included in django-oauth-toolkit in your Django server application as described in the docs ([here is an example from my authserver application](https://github.com/jdelic/authserver/blob/561c245b8680f546a310109b312532939a703dab/authserver/authserver/urls.py#L29)) and follow [OAuth2 Section 4.3.2 (link to spec)](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.3.2). The tl;dr here is: Send `grant_type=password&username=[username]&password=[password]` via `POST` to your `TokenView` URL. Assuming that a `django.contrib.auth.models.User` (or something similar that the Django auth framework can authenticate) exists with username `[username]` and password `[password]`, django-oauth-toolkit should create an access token in your database and reply with it.

No worries!  I want the tests to work correctly for everyone doing development.  I have no permissions problems with sys.prefix using Canopy or brew-installed python, but Anaconda has a different layout.  

What I have been doing in order to the run the tests consistently whether with "python -m unittest discover" or with my pip compatible script “run_all_tests.sh" is this: I manually copied the data files to sys.prefix. That way I can run tests successfully with either local PmagPy or pip PmagPy.  Obviously that isn’t a great solution.  

However, I’m not sure what the best way is to detect whether the tests are being run locally (use os.getcwd() + data_files) or non-locally (use sys.prefix + data_files).  Maybe we could test with regex if os.getcwd() ends with “PmagPy”?  If so, we are local, if not, look in sys.prefix?  We would also need to be careful because some of the tests change directory, but we can probably solve that by making sure we have “os.chdir(pmag_dir)” in each tearDown function so that it gets reset appropriately after every test.  Does that sound like a reasonable solution?  

Name-wise, the only reason I like pmagpy_data_files over data_files is because it is being installed by pip into a location with no clear relationship to PmagPy (for Canopy, ~/Library/Enthought/Canopy_64bit/User).  I wanted the user who comes upon this folder to know what is is for.  For that reason, I would lean towards renaming data_files in PmagPy/ to pmagpy_data_files if we want them to be consistent between the pip data files and the local ones.  Alternatively, we could use  data_files_pmagpy for nice auto-complete purposes.

I'd far prefer that we implement CORS in middleware, than as part of a view pattern.

One thing to note: If an exception occurs in `func` someone could go back in the stack frames and access the local variables of `func`. To prevent this you could modify the wrapper function:

```python
def wraparmor(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        __wraparmor__(func)
        try:
            return func(*args, **kwargs)
+       except Exception as err:
+           raise err
        finally:
            __wraparmor__(func, 1)
    wrapper.__module__ = func.__module__
    wrapper.__name__ = func.__name__
    wrapper.__doc__ = func.__doc__
    wrapper.__dict__.update(func.__dict__)
    func.__refcalls__ = 0
    return wrapper
```
This way it is possible to pass the corresponding frame. It has the downside of a wrong location of the exception and makes debugging more difficult. But if you have some secret variables in a particular function someone could use this decorator instead.

The commands are just used for debugging purpose, so I'd keep them simple. Here is my proposal:

The decoder will accept mvt binary stream from stdin and print out what the function `mvt.encode` returns as json. If the mvt binary is compressed, the user should uncompress it before passing to the decoder.

The encoder will take geojson stream from stdin, a layer name, and a tile coordinate, and print out the mvt binary.

```
mvt_encode layer_name z/x/y < cities.geojson
```

The geometries in the geojson could be in any coordinate system. By default it's WGS84.

Maybe we can add an option `--format` to specify the input format other than geojson, e.g.

```
mvt_decode < 1/2/3.mvt | mvt_encode --format mvt layer_name 1/2/3 > copy/1/2/3.mvt
```

`1/2/3.mvt` and `copy/1/2/3.mvt` will be the same.

This patch uses something complicated (socket listener/client) to allow the step executor to query the master process for available workers. Say, if `-j 4` and there are already two running steps, then the `step_executor` would create a pool of at most `2` processes. This works more or less ok but if two steps ask the same question, they would get the same answer and each start a pool of `2` processes, so although the extreme case if less than `n*n`, it is certainly greater than `n`.

So this patch is used only as a proof-of-concept implementation for this kind of socket-based communication.  Let us see if we should make it work correctly, or remove it because of its complexity.

On the other hand, it's usually very easy to the end user to adapt the network to handle varying sizes. Something like
```python
resnet = torchvision.models.resnet18()
resnet.avgpool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
```
would be enough, and explicit to the user.

On the other hand, it hides a potential problem, which is that the pre-trained models might not work well if too big images are fed to the network, as they were trained to identify patterns at a specific resolution.

I have been thinking a little more about this, and I'm not convinced that dimension names should be static. Let's say you have a general purpose fluid dynamics solver which can work given grid spacing and grid points. It could be used, for example, as a coastal ocean model or a limited area atmosphere model.

Then, there is no way to know in advance what the dimension names will be, and it makes sense that these dimension names be meaningful and should be set in calls to `get_default_state`.

My proposal is that `*_properties` be created at runtime, as it currently is happening, and the 'x' and 'y' dimensions be replaced with appropriate dimension names during runtime.

 'x', 'y' are properly documented dimensions when we don't know what they will be in advance, and they will be replaced with the correct names when these names are known.

One problem with the name "nursery" is it only conveys that this is a place where new tasks can be spawned. But the really important thing about a trio nursery as I understand it, and in particular the thing that distinguishes it from other libraries' APIs for launching concurrent tasks, is that it also bounds the execution of all the tasks launched within it. That is, the nursery doesn't exit until all its child tasks have exited. The "nursery" metaphor doesn't convey this, in fact quite the opposite: most things born in nurseries grow up and leave the nursery to live their adult lives (especially if the nursery is working well and not prematurely killing it's charges :-) ).

I would favor a name that covey's this "boundedness", that no task started by this object will outlive that object. But I don't really have a good suggestion. I like variants of "scope", but @njsmith already rejected "TaskScope". I also like "lifetime", but that doesn't have the whimsy of the current name.
